nodes.name: GitHub Webhook Trigger
nodes.notes:
SETUP REQUIRED: This node listens for incoming webhook events from GitHub.

To connect it:
1. Copy the webhook URL this node generates (shown after you activate the workflow).
2. Go to your GitHub repo → Settings → Webhooks → Add webhook.
3. Paste the URL into 'Payload URL'.
4. Set Content type to 'application/json'.
5. Under 'Which events?', select 'Issues' only.
6. Save the webhook.

Now every time a GitHub issue is opened, edited, or closed, GitHub will send data here instantly.

nodes.name: Filter: Only 'Opened' Issues
nodes.notes:
GitHub sends webhook events for many issue actions: opened, closed, edited, labeled, assigned, etc.

This filter ensures we ONLY proceed when a brand new issue is opened — we don't want to create a duplicate Jira ticket every time someone edits an issue title.

If the action is 'opened' → continue to the next step.
If the action is anything else → the workflow stops here (no Jira ticket created).

nodes.name: Extract & Map Issue Data
nodes.notes:
This is the 'brain' of the workflow. It reads the GitHub issue payload and figures out how to represent it in Jira.

Key decisions made here:

1. TICKET TYPE — Based on GitHub labels:
   - 'bug' or 'defect' → Jira Bug
   - 'feature', 'enhancement', 'story' → Jira Story
   - 'epic' → Jira Epic
   - Anything else → Jira Task (default)

2. PRIORITY — Based on GitHub labels:
   - 'critical', 'urgent', 'p0' → Highest
   - 'high', 'p1' → High
   - 'low', 'p3' → Low
   - Default → Medium

3. SKIP FLAG — Some issues shouldn't become Jira tickets:
   - 'question', 'duplicate', 'wontfix', 'invalid', 'docs'
   - If any of these labels exist, shouldSkip = true

4. DESCRIPTION — A clean Jira-formatted description is built
   that includes the reporter's name, a clickable link back
   to the original GitHub issue, and the full issue body.

CUSTOMIZATION: Edit the label arrays in this code block
to match whatever labels your team actually uses on GitHub.

nodes.name: Filter: Skip Unwanted Labels
nodes.notes:
A second filter that checks the 'shouldSkip' flag set in the previous step.

If the issue has labels like 'question', 'duplicate', 'wontfix', 'invalid', or 'docs', we don't want to pollute Jira with tickets that aren't real work items.

If shouldSkip = false → proceed to create the Jira ticket.
If shouldSkip = true → the workflow stops here quietly.

You can add or remove labels from the skip list by editing the previous 'Extract & Map' node.

nodes.name: Create Jira Ticket
nodes.notes:
SETUP REQUIRED: This node creates the actual Jira ticket.

Before this will work, you need to:
1. Add your Jira credentials in n8n:
   - Go to Credentials → New → Jira Software Cloud API
   - Enter your Jira subdomain (e.g. 'mycompany' from mycompany.atlassian.net)
   - Enter your Atlassian account email
   - Generate an API token at: https://id.atlassian.com/manage-profile/security/api-tokens
   - Paste the token in

2. Set your PROJECT KEY:
   - Replace 'YOUR_JIRA_PROJECT_KEY' with your actual key
   - Find this in Jira: go to your project → look at the URL or ticket IDs
   - e.g. if tickets are named 'DEV-123', your key is 'DEV'

This node will automatically populate:
- Title (from GitHub issue title)
- Description (formatted with reporter, link, and body)
- Issue Type (Bug / Story / Task / Epic based on labels)
- Priority (Highest / High / Medium / Low based on labels)
- A 'github-sync' label so you can filter these in Jira

The Jira ticket key (e.g. DEV-456) is passed to the next step.

nodes.name: Post GitHub Comment with Jira Link
nodes.notes:
SETUP REQUIRED: This node posts a comment back on the original GitHub issue to confirm it's been synced.

The comment will include:
- A clickable link to the Jira ticket that was just created
- The ticket type and priority
- A note to track progress in Jira

This is useful because:
- The person who filed the GitHub issue knows it's been acknowledged
- There's a permanent paper trail linking the two systems
- Anyone browsing GitHub issues can see their Jira status

Before this works:
1. Add GitHub credentials in n8n:
   - Go to Credentials → New → GitHub API
   - Generate a Personal Access Token at: https://github.com/settings/tokens
   - Give it 'repo' scope permissions
   - Paste it into n8n

2. Update the Jira URL:
   - Replace 'YOUR_JIRA_SUBDOMAIN' with your actual Jira subdomain
   - e.g. if your Jira is at 'acme.atlassian.net', use 'acme'

nodes.name: Notify Slack (Optional)
nodes.notes:
OPTIONAL: This node sends a Slack notification to your dev channel whenever a new issue is synced to Jira.

The message includes:
- Clickable link to the GitHub issue
- Clickable link to the new Jira ticket
- Issue type, priority, repo, and reporter

To enable this:
1. Add Slack credentials in n8n:
   - Go to Credentials → New → Slack API
   - Create a Slack App at https://api.slack.com/apps with 'chat:write' scope
   - Copy the Bot OAuth Token

2. Replace 'YOUR_SLACK_CHANNEL_ID' with your channel ID:
   - Right-click a Slack channel → View channel details → Copy channel ID
   - It looks like: C04ABC12345

3. Replace 'YOUR_JIRA_SUBDOMAIN' with your actual subdomain

TO DISABLE: Simply delete this node or disconnect it from the workflow.
The rest of the automation will still work without it.

main.node: Post GitHub Comment with Jira Link
